structured_choices.data_source: Historical transaction data from a single retail chain spanning 60 months (5 years). Raw data: 1M+ individual transactions with timestamp, amount, and item category. Aggregated to monthly revenue figures (primary label). Time period: months 1–60 inclusive. Data quality: check for missing months, outliers from system errors, and reconcile transaction-level sums against reported revenue figures.
structured_choices.label_definition: Monthly revenue is computed as the sum of all transaction amounts within each calendar month. The target variable is the monthly revenue value in dollars (continuous). For model training, each observation is a single month's total revenue. No binning or classification is applied.
structured_choices.split_method: Temporal (time-ordered) split with no shuffling. Months 1–36 (first 3 years) for training. Months 37–48 (years 4, first half) for validation. Months 49–60 (year 5, second half) for test. Justification: Time series forecasting requires temporal integrity to prevent future information leaking into training. A model trained on past data must be validated and tested on strictly future held-out periods. This split mirrors deployment: the model will be retrained periodically on historical data and evaluated on unseen future months. Random shuffling would be invalid for time series.
structured_choices.preprocessing_fit_point: All preprocessing (scaling, imputation, seasonal decomposition) is fit on the training set (months 1–36) only. Fitted transformers (scalers, encoders) are then applied identically to validation (months 37–48) and test (months 49–60) sets. This prevents validation and test data from influencing preprocessing parameters, which would inflate performance estimates.
structured_choices.features: Raw features: (1) Monthly revenue (lagged: t-1, t-3, t-12 to capture short-term trends and annual seasonality). (2) Item category composition (proportion of revenue from top 5 categories, fit on training data). (3) Temporal features: month-of-year (one-hot encoded), is_holiday_month (binary flag for months containing major holidays, defined a priori). (4) Detrended revenue (linear trend removed from training set, applied to val/test). Feature engineering does not use future months or test-set statistics. All lags are constructed such that at time t, only t-1, t-3, t-12 are available.
structured_choices.model: Ensemble combining XGBoost and LSTM. XGBoost component: trained on tabular features (lagged revenue, category mix, temporal indicators). LSTM component: trained on a sequence of the past 12 monthly revenue values as input, predicting the next month's revenue. Ensemble output: equal-weight average of XGBoost and LSTM predictions. Both sub-models are fit on training data only.
structured_choices.baseline: ARIMA(p,d,q) model fit using auto.arima or equivalent, tuned on training data via grid search over p,d,q ∈ {0,1,2} × {0,1} × {0,1,2}, selecting the model with lowest AIC on training set. The ARIMA baseline uses only univariate monthly revenue; no external regressors are added. This ensures a fair comparison: both ensemble and baseline are given tuning effort commensurate with their complexity. The baseline represents the traditional approach mentioned in the hypothesis and is evaluated under identical conditions (same train/val/test split, same primary metric).
structured_choices.primary_metric: Mean Absolute Percentage Error (MAPE) on the test set (months 49–60). MAPE = (1/n) Σ |actual_t - pred_t| / |actual_t| × 100%. Justification: MAPE aligns directly with the stakeholder objective (optimize inventory and promotions based on revenue forecasts) and is scale-independent, making it interpretable as a percentage error across different revenue levels. It penalizes both over- and under-prediction equally in percentage terms, which is appropriate for revenue planning. Test-set MAPE is the primary comparison metric between ensemble and ARIMA.
secondary_metrics: Mean Absolute Error (MAE) in dollars on test set — interpretable for absolute planning errors.
Root Mean Squared Error (RMSE) on test set — penalizes large forecast errors more heavily, relevant if large misforecasts are costly.
Directional accuracy on test set (% of months where the forecasted direction of change matches actual change) — useful for qualitative decision-making.
Residual autocorrelation (Ljung-Box test p-value) on test residuals for both models — diagnostic, checks whether systematic patterns remain unpredicted.
structured_choices.model_selection_data: Hyperparameter tuning for XGBoost (learning rate, max_depth, n_estimators) and LSTM (hidden units, dropout, learning rate) is performed via grid search on the validation set (months 37–48). The model configuration (including ensemble weights, if weighted differently than equal) is selected to minimize validation MAPE. The test set (months 49–60) is not touched during tuning or selection; it is reserved for final evaluation only.
structured_choices.test_set_policy: The test set is used once, at the very end, after all model selection is complete. Test-set predictions are generated for months 49–60 using the fitted ensemble and baseline ARIMA models. Test MAPE and secondary metrics are computed and reported. No retraining, parameter adjustment, or model selection occurs after test evaluation.
structured_choices.confound_controls: Known confound: external market events (economic downturns, pandemics, supply chain disruptions) that affect consumer spending independent of model quality. Mitigation: (1) Document any major known events in months 1–60 and record them in a log. (2) Sensitivity analysis: refit and evaluate both models on test data stratified by event periods vs. normal periods to assess whether performance degrades uniformly or shows interaction effects. (3) Scope the claim: the experiment establishes whether the ensemble outperforms ARIMA *on this historical data under these conditions*; it does not establish robustness to novel external events outside the 5-year window. (4) If a major event occurs within the test window (months 49–60), document whether prediction errors spike and whether the ensemble and ARIMA respond differently. Report this separately from the primary metric. Confound does not invalidate the comparison if both models are evaluated on the same data; it constrains the generalizability of the findings.

design_narrative:
# Experiment Design: Ensemble vs. ARIMA for Retail Revenue Forecasting

## 1. Data and Labels

The experiment uses 60 months of historical transaction data from a single retail chain. Individual transactions (1M+) are aggregated into monthly revenue figures, which become the target variable for forecasting. Before analysis, data quality is verified: check for missing months, reconcile transaction sums against reported revenue records, and flag outliers attributable to system errors or data entry issues. The final dataset comprises 60 monthly revenue observations spanning years 1–5.

## 2. Temporal Split Strategy

Since this is a time series forecasting task, the data is split chronologically without shuffling. Months 1–36 form the training set, months 37–48 form the validation set, and months 49–60 form the test set. This 60%–20%–20% split is chosen to reflect how the model will be deployed: trained on historical data and evaluated on unseen future periods. Temporal ordering prevents look-ahead bias and ensures that validation and test represent a realistic forecasting scenario. Any shuffling would break the temporal structure and inflate reported accuracy.

## 3. Feature Engineering

Features are constructed from the training set and applied consistently to validation and test sets:

- **Lagged revenue**: t-1, t-3, t-12 (short-term trend and annual seasonality)
- **Category composition**: proportion of monthly revenue from top 5 item categories (fit on training data distribution)
- **Temporal indicators**: month-of-year (one-hot encoded) and a binary flag for holiday months (defined a priori)
- **Detrended revenue**: linear trend fitted on months 1–36, subtracted from all monthly values

All preprocessing (scaling, trend removal, category definition) is fit exclusively on the training set and applied to validation and test to prevent data leakage.

## 4. Models and Baseline

The ensemble combines two sub-models:
- **XGBoost**: trained on tabular features (lags, category mix, temporal indicators)
- **LSTM**: trained on sequences of 12 prior months, predicting the next month

The ensemble output is the equal-weight average of both predictions. The baseline is an ARIMA(p,d,q) model fit on training data using auto-selection (grid search over p,d,q ∈ {0,1,2} × {0,1} × {0,1,2}, selecting the lowest AIC). The ARIMA model uses only univariate revenue; no external regressors are added. Both ensemble and baseline receive equivalent tuning effort and are evaluated identically.

## 5. Model Selection and Validation

Hyperparameters for XGBoost (learning rate, max_depth, n_estimators) and LSTM (hidden units, dropout) are tuned via grid search on the validation set (months 37–48), optimizing for validation MAPE. The final model configuration is locked before test evaluation. The test set is held completely untouched during all tuning and selection phases.

## 6. Evaluation Metrics

**Primary metric**: Mean Absolute Percentage Error (MAPE) on the test set. MAPE directly aligns with the stakeholder objective of accurate monthly revenue forecasting for inventory and promotional planning. It is scale-independent and interpretable as a percentage error across different revenue levels.

**Secondary metrics**:
- MAE (dollars) — absolute forecast error
- RMSE — penalizes large errors
- Directional accuracy — percentage of months where forecast direction matches actual
- Ljung-Box test on residuals — checks for systematic unpredicted patterns

## 7. Confounds and Scope

External market events (economic downturns, pandemics) may affect consumer spending patterns. These confounds do not invalidate the comparison if both models are evaluated on the same data; however, they constrain the claim. The experiment establishes whether the ensemble outperforms ARIMA *on this historical dataset under observed conditions*. To assess robustness, sensitivity analysis is performed: test performance is stratified by whether months fall within documented event periods or normal periods. Major events in the test window are logged and reported separately.

## 8. Test Set Evaluation

Once model selection is complete, both the fitted ensemble and baseline ARIMA model generate predictions for months 49–60 (test set). MAPE and secondary metrics are computed. No retraining or parameter adjustment occurs after test evaluation. The test metric comparison directly answers whether the ensemble hypothesis holds on this data.
